title: First Aid & Safety
subtitle: Quick-reference for coaches · UKA-sourced
disclaimer: Quick reference only. Not a substitute for proper first aid training. If unsure, call 999. Source: UKA First Aid Essentials in Sport and Active Leisure (Modules 1-3).

scenarios.name: Basic Life Support · CPR
scenarios.summary: Unresponsive person, not breathing normally. Chest compressions keep oxygenated blood circulating.

scenarios.name: Choking
scenarios.summary: Object blocking airway. Person can't speak, cough or breathe normally.

scenarios.name: Anaphylaxis
scenarios.summary: Severe allergic reaction — nuts, stings, medications. Airway swelling can be fatal in minutes.

scenarios.name: Shock
scenarios.summary: Body's circulation collapsing. Caused by severe bleeding, infection, burns, heart attack, severe vomiting/diarrhoea, anaphylaxis.

scenarios.name: Stroke · F.A.S.T.
scenarios.summary: Blood vessel burst or clot in the brain. Brain is being starved of oxygen — every minute counts.

scenarios.name: Heart attack
scenarios.summary: Blocked blood vessel to the heart muscle. Chest pain is the classic sign.

scenarios.name: Seizure
scenarios.summary: Electrical disturbance in the brain (e.g. epilepsy). From minor twitching to full convulsions and collapse.

scenarios.name: Drowning
scenarios.summary: Water has blocked the airway. Can occur in surprisingly small amounts of fluid.

scenarios.name: Severe bleeding
scenarios.summary: Heavy blood loss from a wound. Direct pressure stops blood flow; without it, shock and death follow.

scenarios.name: Severe burns and scalds
scenarios.summary: Dry-heat burns or wet-heat scalds. If it's hot, cool it.

scenarios.name: Broken bones
scenarios.summary: Suspected fracture. The larger the bone, the more dangerous (internal bleeding risk).

scenarios.name: Asthma attack
scenarios.summary: Airways narrow — hard to breathe. Most children with asthma carry a blue (ventolin/salbutamol) inhaler.

scenarios.name: Low blood sugar (hypo)
scenarios.summary: Hypoglycaemia. Common in diabetic children but can happen to anyone — missed meal, over-exertion, illness.

scenarios.name: Electrocution · electrical burn
scenarios.summary: Electric shock from mains or equipment. Can stop the heart. Internal burns may not be visible.

scenarios.name: Sprain or strain · RICE
scenarios.summary: Sprain = ligament tear (joint). Strain = muscle/tendon tear. Common in sport.

scenarios.name: Nose bleed
scenarios.summary: Common in children. Rarely serious. Usually stops with simple pressure.

scenarios.name: Insect bites and stings
scenarios.summary: Midges, mosquitoes, ticks, fleas, wasps, bees. Usually minor — but watch for anaphylaxis.

scenarios.name: Fainting
scenarios.summary: Brief loss of consciousness from reduced blood flow to the brain. Usually recovers within a minute.

scenarios.name: Heatstroke
scenarios.summary: Body's temperature regulation failing. Risk during hot weather, strenuous exercise, poor adaptation to heat.

scenarios.name: Hypothermia
scenarios.summary: Core body temperature dropping. Risk in cold, wet, windy conditions. Children lose heat faster than adults.

scenarios.name: Dehydration
scenarios.summary: Common in sport, especially high temperatures or altitude. Body loses water + important blood salts.

scenarios.name: Sunburn
scenarios.summary: Skin damage from UV exposure. "If it's hot, cool it."
